inspect-unstaged-changes-with-diff.label: Inspect Unstaged Changes with git diff
inspect-unstaged-changes-with-diff.summary: Read the exact lines changed in tracked files before deciding what belongs in the next snapshot.

sketch0.title: See the Edit, Not Just the Filename

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Read One Unstaged Edit
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The community site heading changed in `index.html`. Inspect only that file’s unstaged diff.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: git diff shows working-tree changes that have not been staged.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash set -eu rm -rf community-site mkdir -p community-site git -C community-site init -q -b main git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner" git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local" cd community-site printf %s '

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline' cd community-site printf %s '

Neighborhood Hub

' > index.html cd ..

Community Hub

' > index.html cd .. git -C community-site add -A git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline' cd community-site printf %s '

Neighborhood Hub

' > index.html cd ..

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: Compare a Style Change
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

The site palette changed in `styles.css`. Inspect that file without including the unrelated README edit.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: A path-limited diff narrows review to one file without altering any changes.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: navy;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Site
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: teal;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Website
' > README.md
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash

git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: navy;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Site
' > README.md
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'color: teal;
' > styles.css
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s '# Community Website
' > README.md
cd ..

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.title: List the Files That Changed
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.prompt:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Two tracked files changed. Use the name-only diff view first, then inspect `app.js` in detail.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.
try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.hint: Read the repository state first, then use the Git command that changes only the requested boundary.

help.concept: The name-only view reveals the scope of an edit before a detailed diff explains one file.
help.hint_1: Use the exact file or repository named in the task; avoid broad commands that include unrelated work.
help.hint_2: After the action, run the requested inspection command to confirm the final state.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "light";
' > config.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = true;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "dark";
' > config.js
cd ..

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf community-site
mkdir -p community-site
git -C community-site init -q -b main
git -C community-site config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C community-site config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = false;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "light";
' > config.js
cd ..
git -C community-site add -A
git -C community-site commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd community-site
printf %s 'const open = true;
' > app.js
cd ..
cd community-site
printf %s 'export const theme = "dark";
' > config.js
cd ..

try_inspect_unstaged_changes_with_diff_s.instructions:
The terminal opens inside the prepared `community-site/` repository.

Two tracked files changed. Use the name-only diff view first, then inspect `app.js` in detail.

Type each command in the terminal and press Enter.

0.message: Do not use destructive history-rewriting commands in this course.
Run `git diff --name-only`, then check your answer again.

1.message: Run `git diff -- app.js`, then check your answer again.

multi-choice-1.title: Understanding git diff
multi-choice-1.prompt: What does the `git diff` command do?
multi-choice-1.hint: Think about what changes `git diff` shows.

help.concept: `git diff` shows changes in the working directory that are not yet staged.
help.hint_1: `git diff` compares the working directory with the staging area.
help.hint_2: It highlights changes that are not yet staged for commit.

options.b: Displays changes in the working directory not yet staged

sketch0.title: See the Edit, Not Just the Filename
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
Status tells you which tracked files changed; `git diff` shows the unstaged line changes inside them.

```bash
git diff
```

Suppose `menu.txt` changed from `Tea` to `Tea and coffee`. The diff marks the old line with `-` and the new line with `+`. Those symbols describe the comparison; they are not characters stored in the file.

Run the command before staging so you know exactly what you are proposing.

sketch1.title: Read a Diff Without Guessing
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
A diff begins with file headers, then shows a small block around the changed lines. Focus on three questions: which file is shown, which lines were removed, and which lines were added.

```diff
-Tea
+Tea and coffee
```

Context lines may appear without a plus or minus. They help you locate the edit but are unchanged. Reading the body before the headers keeps the first review approachable.

sketch2.title: Catch Unrelated Work Early
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
A file can contain more than one edit. Before staging, scan the diff for changes that belong to different purposes.

If `menu.txt` updates refreshments while `footer.txt` fixes a phone number, those changes may deserve separate commits. Git does not decide that boundary for you; the diff gives you the evidence to decide.

Focused history begins before `git add`, while the work is still easy to separate.

sketch3.title: Make Review the Default
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
A calm workflow repeats the same questions: `git status --short` for the file-level map, then `git diff` for the line-level details.

Reviewing first prevents accidental debug text, unfinished wording, and unrelated edits from entering the staging area. It also makes commit messages easier to write because you already understand the change.

When the diff matches the intended task, you are ready to stage.
